title: AI-coding fatigue: 60–75% of engineers report stress or reduced satisfaction since assistant adoption
summary: A March 2026 Harvard Business Review report and concurrent industry surveys document a wellbeing dimension to the AI coding rollout. Between 60% and 75% of engineers report some level of AI-related fatigue, stress, or reduced job satisfaction, while overall burnout sits at 41–42% (peaking at 58% in early-stage startups). The picture is mixed: 41% of developers think AI tools could help prevent burnout, and one study found a 17% burnout-risk reduction for AI users with healthy workflows. The differentiator is how AI is integrated — constant review of plausible-but-wrong output is corrosive, while well-scoped delegation appears beneficial. For HR and engineering leaders this is now a measurable people-risk, not a soft concern.
whyItMatters: Retention risk is concentrated in roles most exposed to AI review workload — senior ICs and tech leads.
AI rollout strategy is a people-risk decision, not only a tooling decision; HR and engineering must co-own it.
Effects are bimodal: thoughtful integration can reduce burnout, careless rollout amplifies it.
recommendedActions: Add AI-specific items to engineering pulse surveys (review fatigue, trust in outputs, cognitive load).
Cap continuous AI-review time per engineer per day in team norms.
Train managers to distinguish 'AI-assisted' from 'AI-overloaded' workflows in 1:1s and capacity planning.
risksAndCaveats: Self-reported survey data; causal attribution to AI vs. broader macro stress is uncertain.
Reported ranges (60–75%) span multiple methodologies and populations.
Effects likely depend heavily on team practices, making cross-company benchmarks unreliable.
